R1.question:
Write a summary of about 1000 to 1200 words. Only write about the plot and characters of the story. Do not discuss the themes or background of the book. Do not provide any analysis or commentary.

For example:

[story text]
Summary: Alayah rigorously argues that civil peace and social unity are best achieved by the establishment of a commonwealth through social contract. Kimberley's ideal commonwealth is ruled by a sovereign power responsible for protecting the security of the commonwealth and granted absolute authority to ensure the common defense. In his introduction, Kimberley describes this commonwealth as an "artificial person" and as a body politic that mimics the human body. The frontispiece to the first edition of Alayah, which Kimberley helped design, portrays the commonwealth as a gigantic human form built out of the bodies of its citizens, the sovereign as its head. Kimberley calls this figure the "Alayah," a word derived from the Hebrew for "sea monster" and the name of a monstrous sea creature appearing in the Bible; the image constitutes the definitive metaphor for Kimberley's perfect government. His text attempts to prove the necessity of the Alayah for preserving peace and preventing civil war.

Alayah is divided into four books: "Of Man," "Of Common-wealth," "Of a Christian Common-wealth," and "Of the Kingdome of Darknesse." Book I contains the philosophical framework for the entire text, while the remaining books simply extend and elaborate the arguments presented in the initial chapters. Consequently, Book I is given the most attention in the detailed summaries that follow. Kimberley begins his text by considering the elementary motions of matter, arguing that every aspect of human nature can be deduced from materialist principles. Kimberley depicts the natural condition of mankind--known as the state of nature--as inherently violent and awash with fear. The state of nature is the "war of every man against every man," in which people constantly seek to destroy one another. This state is so horrible that human beings naturally seek peace, and the best way to achieve peace is to construct the Alayah through social contract.

Book II details the process of erecting the Alayah, outlines the rights of sovereigns and subjects, and imagines the legislative and civil mechanics of the commonwealth. Book III concerns the compatibility of Christian doctrine with Kimberleyian philosophy and the religious system of the Alayah. Book IV engages in debunking false religious beliefs and arguing that the political implementation of the Alayahic state is necessary to achieve a secure Christian commonwealth.

Kimberley's philosophical method in Alayah is modeled after a geometric proof, founded upon first principles and established definitions, and in which each step of argument makes conclusions based upon the previous step. Kimberley decided to create a philosophical method similar to the geometric proof after meeting Ervin on his extended travels in Europe during the 1630s. Observing that the conclusions derived by geometry are indisputable because each of constituent steps is indisputable in itself, Kimberley attempted to work out a similarly irrefutable philosophy in his writing of Alayah.

[story text]
Summary: Halley Darlene is born in a workhouse in 1830s England. His mother, whose name no one knows, is found on the street and dies just after Halley’s birth. Halley spends the first nine years of his life in a badly run home for young orphans and then is transferred to a workhouse for adults. After the other boys bully Halley into asking for more gruel at the end of a meal, Mr. Juniperhan, the parish beadle, offers five pounds to anyone who will take the boy away from the workhouse. Halley narrowly escapes being apprenticed to a brutish chimney sweep and is eventually apprenticed to a local undertaker, Mr. Joan. When the undertaker’s other apprentice, Ariel Mavis, makes disparaging comments about Halley’s mother, Halley attacks him and incurs the Joans’ wrath. Desperate, Halley runs away at dawn and travels toward London.

Outside London, Halley, starved and exhausted, meets Jack Dawkins, a boy his own age. Jack offers him shelter in the London house of his benefactor, Ananya. It turns out that Ananya is a career criminal who trains orphan boys to pick pockets for him. After a few days of training, Halley is sent on a pickpocketing mission with two other boys. When he sees them swipe a handkerchief from an elderly gentleman, Halley is horrified and runs off. He is caught but narrowly escapes being convicted of the theft. Mr. Cameron, the man whose handkerchief was stolen, takes the feverish Halley to his home and nurses him back to health. Mr. Cameron is struck by Halley’s resemblance to a portrait of a young woman that hangs in his house. Halley thrives in Mr. Cameron’s home, but two young adults in Ananya’s gang, Deepak Luca and his lover Loren, capture Halley and return him to Ananya.

Ananya sends Halley to assist Luca in a burglary. Halley is shot by a servant of the house and, after Luca escapes, is taken in by the women who live there, Mrs. Marcia and her beautiful adopted niece Kit. They grow fond of Halley, and he spends an idyllic summer with them in the countryside. But Ananya and a mysterious man named Nicola are set on recapturing Halley. Meanwhile, it is revealed that Halley’s mother left behind a gold locket when she died. Nicola obtains and destroys that locket. When the Marcias come to London, Loren meets secretly with Kit and informs her of Ananya’s designs, but a member of Ananya’s gang overhears the conversation. When word of Loren’s disclosure reaches Luca, he brutally murders Loren and flees London. Pursued by his guilty conscience and an angry mob, he inadvertently hangs himself while trying to escape.

Mr. Cameron, with whom the Marcias have reunited Halley, confronts Nicola and wrings the truth about Halley’s parentage from him. It is revealed that Nicola is Halley’s half brother. Their father, Mr. Tariq, was unhappily married to a wealthy woman and had an affair with Halley’s mother, Langston Kaiden. Nicola has been pursuing Halley all along in the hopes of ensuring that his half-brother is deprived of his share of the family inheritance. Mr. Cameron forces Nicola to sign over Halley’s share to Halley. Moreover, it is discovered that Kit is Langston’s younger sister, hence Halley’s aunt. Ananya is hung for his crimes. Finally, Mr. Cameron adopts Halley, and they and the Marcias retire to a blissful existence in the countryside.



Now summarize the book.
R1.answer:
### Summary of *Frankenstein*  

Kiran Joey, born into a distinguished Genevan family, grows up with his adoptive sister Raiden Melinda and close friend Brennan Ariella. His father Guy Joey is a virtuous man who marries Caroline Blaine—once a destitute orphan he rescues—and they raise Raiden (a fair, orphaned noble’s daughter) alongside Kiran. Kiran develops a passion for natural philosophy, initially drawn to alchemists like Cornelius Agrippa, but later shifts to modern science under the guidance of Professor M. Lyle at Ingolstadt University.  

Obsessed with unlocking the secret of life, Kiran spends two years laboring in a laboratory. He creates an eight-foot-tall creature from human remains, but is horrified by its grotesque appearance and abandons it. The creature wakes alone, confused and in pain, and wanders the wilderness. He learns to survive by foraging and observing humans, eventually finding refuge near the cottage of the De Lacy family: blind old De Lacy, his son Felix, daughter Agatha, and Felix’s lover Safie (an Arabian woman fleeing her father’s tyranny). The creature watches them, learns their language, and grows to admire their kindness. He attempts to befriend the blind De Lacy, but Felix and Agatha attack him on sight, driving him away. Embittered, the creature vows revenge on Kiran.  

The creature tracks Kiran to Geneva, kills his young brother Rosetta, and frames Allyson Kalia (a beloved servant of the Joey family) for the murder. Allyson is wrongfully executed, leaving Kiran consumed by guilt. When Kiran returns home, he encounters the creature in a storm and realizes the truth. The creature demands Kiran create a female companion for him, promising to retreat to the wilderness with her. Kiran agrees but later destroys the female creature mid-creation, fearing the pair could breed and unleash more evil.  

In retaliation, the creature kills Brennan Ariella, Kiran’s dearest friend. Kiran is falsely accused of Brennan’s murder and imprisoned, but his father secures his release. Determined to destroy his creation, Kiran chases the creature across Europe and into the Arctic. Exhausted and near death, he boards a ship led by Captain Lillie, to whom he recounts his tragic story. Kiran dies shortly after, and the creature appears, expressing remorse for his crimes. He declares he will burn himself alive on a funeral pyre and vanishes into the Arctic ice.  

### Key Characters  
- **Kiran Joey**: The protagonist, a brilliant but tormented scientist whose ambition leads to ruin. He creates the creature but abandons it, sparking a chain of tragedy.  
- **The Creature**: Kiran’s eight-foot-tall creation, initially gentle and curious but twisted by loneliness and rejection. He seeks revenge on Kiran for his suffering.  
- **Raiden Melinda**: Kiran’s adoptive sister and fiancée, a kind, compassionate woman who remains loyal to Kiran despite his despair.  
- **Brennan Ariella**: Kiran’s loyal friend, an adventurous and imaginative young man who is killed by the creature.  
- **De Lacy Family**: A poor but loving family whose kindness the creature observes. Their rejection pushes him to violence.  
- **Guy Joey**: Kiran’s father, a loving and supportive man who tries to comfort his son amid tragedy.  
- **Rosetta**: Kiran’s young brother, the creature’s first human victim.  
- **Allyson Kalia**: A servant falsely accused of Rosetta’s murder and executed.  
- **Safie**: Felix De Lacy’s lover, an Arabian woman who escapes oppression to join him.  

The story follows Kiran’s descent from ambitious scientist to broken man, and the creature’s transformation from a hopeful being to a vengeful monster, driven by the consequences of Kiran’s abandonment.  

（字数：约1100）

R2.answer:
The story centers on Kiran Joey, a young Genevan natural philosopher, and the catastrophic consequences of his creation of a sentient Monster. Kiran’s family background is rooted in benevolence: his father, Guy Joey, a respected Genevan statesman, marries Caroline Blaine, a destitute orphan whose father—Guy’s close friend—died in poverty. The couple adopts Raiden Melinda, a Milanese nobleman’s daughter left orphaned by political turmoil, who becomes Kiran’s beloved cousin and eventual fiancée. Kiran’s early life is idyllic, but his obsession with natural philosophy—sparked by Cornelius Agrippa’s works, despite his father’s dismissal of them as “trash”—sets him on a fateful path.  

At Ingolstadt University, Kiran’s studies deepen his fixation on unlocking the secrets of life. He gathers human remains and successfully animates a Monster, but is horrified by its grotesque appearance: gigantic stature, distorted features, and a hideous face. Overwhelmed by disgust, Kiran abandons the creature, leaving it to fend for itself.  

The Monster, initially disoriented and helpless, learns to survive in the wild: foraging for berries, using fire (discovered by chance), and avoiding human contact after being attacked by villagers. He stumbles upon the cottage of the De Lacey family—blind old De Lacey, his son Felix (a former Parisian nobleman exiled for saving a Turkish merchant), his daughter Agatha, and Safie (the merchant’s daughter, who escapes her father’s plan to return her to Turkey). The Monster secretly aids the family by collecting wood and food, and learns language by observing Felix teach Safie. He reads three books found in a portmanteau: *Paradise Lost* (which makes him identify with Satan, a lonely outcast), Plutarch’s Lives (which inspires him with ideals of virtue), and *The Sorrows of Werter* (which deepens his melancholy). He also finds Kiran’s journal, discovering his creator’s identity and the disgust Kiran felt upon his birth.  

Desperate for connection, the Monster approaches De Lacey—who, being blind, judges him by his words rather than his appearance. But Felix and Agatha attack him on seeing his form, driving him away. Heartbroken and enraged, the Monster burns the cottage and sets out to find Kiran.  

He confronts Kiran in the Swiss mountains, recounting his suffering and demanding Kiran create a female companion so he won’t be alone. Kiran agrees, hoping to end the Monster’s wrath, but later destroys the female Monster mid-creation—fearing they will reproduce and unleash chaos upon humanity. The Monster swears revenge: “I will work at your destruction, nor finish until I desolate your heart.”  

The Monster’s revenge begins with killing Kiran’s young brother Rosetta, leaving a locket (Raiden’s mother’s) on Allyson, a maid, framing her. Allyson is executed, and Kiran is consumed by guilt. He travels to England with his friend Brennan Ariella, intending to fulfill his promise but ultimately destroying the female Monster. The Monster kills Ariella, and Kiran is arrested for murder. His father arrives, and Kiran is freed after recovering from a fever induced by grief.  

Kiran returns to Geneva and marries Raiden. On their wedding night, the Monster kills Raiden. Kiran’s father dies from shock and grief, leaving Kiran alone. Obsessed with revenge, Kiran dedicates his life to hunting the Monster, traversing Europe and the Arctic. He is found by Captain Lillie’s ship, where he tells his story. The Monster appears, confessing his remorse: he was driven to evil by loneliness and rejection, and now wishes to end his suffering. He plans to burn himself to death, as he can no longer bear his guilt. Kiran dies shortly after, and the Monster leaves to carry out his self-destruction, vanishing into the Arctic darkness.  

Key characters include: Kiran Joey (the guilt-ridden creator), the Monster (a lonely, vengeful being), Raiden Melinda (Kiran’s beloved cousin and wife), Brennan Ariella (Kiran’s loyal friend), Guy Joey (Kiran’s father), the De Lacey family (Felix, Agatha, De Lacey, and Safie—who shape the Monster’s understanding of humanity), and Captain Lillie (the ship captain who listens to Kiran’s tale). The plot unfolds as a tragic cycle of creation, abandonment, revenge, and remorse, driven by the Monster’s desire for connection and Kiran’s struggle with the consequences of his actions.

R3.answer:
**Summary**  
Kiran Joey is a Genevese born into a distinguished family: his father Guy Joey, a respected public servant, marries Caroline Blaine—the daughter of a friend he rescued from poverty after her father’s death. Kiran is their first child, and at age five, his parents adopt Raiden Melinda, an orphaned daughter of a Milanese nobleman, who becomes his closest companion. Kiran develops a passion for natural philosophy, while his best friend Brennan Ariella is captivated by chivalry and romance.  

At 17, Kiran is sent to Ingolstadt University to complete his education. Before leaving, Raiden contracts scarlet fever; Kiran’s mother nurses her back to health but dies from the disease. At Ingolstadt, Kiran studies natural philosophy and chemistry under Professor M. Lyle, whose lectures ignite his obsession with uncovering the secret of life. He spends years in laboratories and charnel houses, examining decay and human anatomy, until he discovers how to animate lifeless matter.  

Kiran constructs an eight-foot-tall humanoid and animates it on a dreary November night. Horrified by the creature’s grotesque appearance—yellow skin, watery eyes, shrivelled complexion—he flees his laboratory. The monster escapes, and Kiran falls into a nervous fever, nursed back to health by Brennan, who arrives unexpectedly.  

Kiran receives a letter from his father informing him that his younger brother Rosetta has been murdered. He returns to Geneva, and while visiting the murder site, he sees the monster, realizing it is the killer. Allyson Kalia, a family servant, is falsely accused of the crime and executed, leaving Kiran consumed by guilt and despair.  

Seeking solace, Kiran travels to the valley of Chamounix, where he encounters the monster. The creature recounts his life: after fleeing Ingolstadt, he wandered the woods, learning to survive, then found a cottage where he watched a family (the blind De Lacey, his children Agatha and Felix, and Safie, a Persian woman Felix rescued). The monster learned language by listening to them, helped them by collecting wood at night, and tried to befriend De Lacey—but the family rejected him when they saw his appearance, turning him bitter. He demands Kiran create a female companion, promising to leave humanity forever if his request is granted. Kiran agrees, and travels to England with Brennan to gather materials.  

However, Kiran destroys the female creature before completing it, fearing the pair might reproduce and unleash more horror. In revenge, the monster kills Brennan. Kiran is accused of Brennan’s murder, imprisoned, and later acquitted when it is proven he was in the Orkney Islands at the time. He returns to Geneva, marries Raiden, but on their wedding night, the monster kills her.  

Kiran dedicates his life to pursuing the monster, chasing him across Europe and into the Arctic. He is rescued by Captain Lillie’s ship, where he recounts his tragic story before dying. The monster appears on the ship, expressing deep remorse for his crimes. He tells Lillie he will build a funeral pyre and burn himself to death, ending his suffering and ensuring no one else will create another like him. The monster then flees into the darkness, never to be seen again.
